coredata.dc:title: A review on the chemical and biological sensing applications of silver/carbon dots nanocomposites with their interaction mechanisms
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science

Gedda, Gangaraju

Habibati,
Gollavelli, Ganesh

coredata.dc:description: The development of new nanocomposites has a significant impact on modern instrumentation and analytical methods for chemical analysis. Due to their unique properties, carbon dots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), distinguished by their unique physical, electrochemical, and optical properties, have captivated significant attention. Thus, combining AgNPs and CDs may produce Ag/CDs nanocomposites with improved performances than the individual material. This comprehensive review offers an in-depth exploration of the synthesis, formation mechanism, properties, and the recent surge in chemical and biological sensing applications of Ag/CDs with their sensing mechanisms. Detailed insights into synthesis methods to produce Ag/CDs are unveiled, followed by information on their physicochemical and optical properties. The crux of this review lies in its spotlight on the diverse landscape of chemical and biological sensing applications of Ag/CDs, with a particular focus on fluorescence, electrochemical, colorimetric, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, and surface plasmon resonance sensing techniques. The elucidation of sensing mechanisms of the nanocomposites with various target analytes adds depth to the discussion. Finally, this review culminates with a concise summary and a glimpse into future perspectives of Ag/CDs aiming to achieve highly efficient and enduring Ag/CDs for various applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new nanocomposites has a significant impact on modern instrumentation and analytical methods for chemical analysis. Due to their unique properties, carbon dots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), distinguished by their unique physical, electrochemical, and optical properties, have captivated significant attention. Thus, combining AgNPs and CDs may produce Ag/CDs nanocomposites with improved performances than the individual material. This comprehensive review offers an in-depth exploration of the synthesis, formation mechanism, properties, and the recent surge in chemical and biological sensing applications of Ag/CDs with their sensing mechanisms. Detailed insights into synthesis methods to produce Ag/CDs are unveiled, followed by information on their physicochemical and optical properties. The crux of this review lies in its spotlight on the diverse landscape of chemical and biological sensing applications of Ag/CDs, with a particular focus on fluorescence, electrochemical, colorimetric, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, and surface plasmon resonance sensing techniques. The elucidation of sensing mechanisms of the nanocomposites with various target analytes adds depth to the discussion. Finally, this review culminates with a concise summary and a glimpse into future perspectives of Ag/CDs aiming to achieve highly efficient and enduring Ag/CDs for various applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of carbon nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs), graphene, carbon nanofibers, carbon nanotubes, and graphene oxide is a significant research area in various branches of modern science and technology [1,2]. Among several types of carbon (C) nanomaterials, CDs have gained widespread attention due to their unique physical and chemical properties [3,4]. CDs, unlike inorganic quantum dots (QDs) that may contain harmful elements like Pb or Cd, are appealing due to their easy synthesis, cost-effectiveness, environmentally friendly nature, and non-toxic composition [5,6]. Typically, CDs are composed of an amorphous sp2-conjugated nanocrystalline core and contain an abundance of oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) moieties such as carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), carbonyl (-C=O), and amino (−NH2) groups on their surface. In addition, their excellent electronic properties as electron acceptors and donors enable them to produce fluorescence (FL) [7,8]. CDs are gaining importance in nanotechnology, particularly in FL-based sensing applications, due to their distinctive optical and electronic properties, including customizable FL, excellent stability, straightforward surface modification, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, and high compatibility with biological systems [9].

CDs represent a convergence of QDs and molecules, heightening their allure. This has propelled CDs into a swiftly evolving realm of interdisciplinary exploration, encompassing photophysics, quantum chemistry, and practical applications [10]. CDs play a crucial role in chemical and biological sensing because of their unique characteristics, such as excitation-dependent emission, increased resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, and enhanced water solubility [11]. They underpin a spectrum of sensors targeting molecules, pH levels, ions, and biological substances, some particularly detectable through electrostatic interactions with CDs [12]. Furthermore, CDs are gaining recognition as strong candidates for bioimaging because they have unique FL properties, resist photobleaching, are less toxic to cells, and are more soluble in water [13]. In the near-infrared (NIR) range, the FL emission of CDs holds special importance for in vivo optical imaging, given the background interference from tissues in the NIR “water window” [12]. Unlike heavy metal QDs, CDs, with their strong absorbance, can make up for lower FL output in bioimaging.

Additionally, CDs have the potential to serve as drug carriers and gene delivery, owing to their strong FL, chemical stability, low toxicity, cost-effective synthesis, easy surface modification, small size, and enhanced biocompatibility [14]. The inherent FL of CDs enables the monitoring of the drug and gene delivery route, facilitating the targeted transportation and elimination of a specific drug for precise therapeutic applications. Moreover, in response to the challenges posed by environmental pollution and the energy resource crisis, CDs have found utility as photocatalysts in processes such as dye degradation, solar water splitting, and CO2 conversion [15]. CDs show great promise in photocatalytic reactions due to their unique qualities, including better water solubility, low toxicity, and strong chemical stability [16]. In addition, CDs possess capabilities for up-conversion FL and photoinduced electron transfer, which has garnered considerable interest in their application in photocatalyst design.

On the other hand, AgNPs with their unique physicochemical properties have been used in various applications including biosensors, drug delivery, phototherapy, and energy storage applications [17,18]. Specifically, the surface functionalization of AgNPs enables the sensing of a wide variety of molecules and biomolecules, including drugs, oligonucleotides, antibodies, and proteins [19–21]. Usually, the chemical reduction method was used to produce AgNPs by using some reducing agents such as amines, citrates, borohydride, and ascorbic acid [22–24]. To avoid colloidal agglomeration, the reduction process in the presence of stabilizing/protecting/capping agents is necessary. Furthermore, hydrothermal, microwave, and photosynthesis methods were employed to improve the reduction process of ionic Ag+ into metallic Ag [25,26]. However, these methods are limited by using multiple toxic and expensive chemicals or by time-consuming synthesis and purification steps. Hence, it is interesting to investigate economical and eco-friendly dual-functional compounds that can serve as reducing and stabilizing agents for AgNPs.

CDs meet primary conditions for the synthesis of AgNPs. As a result of the abundance of functional groups (hydroxyl and phenolic groups) on the CDs surface, CDs can operate as reducing and stabilizing agents to prevent AgNPs from aggregation [27,28]. Integrating two nanomaterials into a nanocomposite boosts their physicochemical properties [29,30]. The resultant Ag/CDs nanocomposites formed by modifying AgNPs and CDs have a significant role as chemical and biological sensors for detecting important analytes. Ag-CDs are most commonly used as FL, electrochemical (EC), colorimetric (COL), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopic (SERS), and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors [31–35]. Fig. 1 shows the sensor types with their various advantages. This review paper highlights a comprehensive examination of reports on the utilization of Ag/CDs as chemical and biological sensors, spanning from their inception to the present day. A complete guide on synthesizing Ag/CDs with customizable properties towards various sensing applications is provided. Additionally, the primary roles of CDs in Ag/CDs formation strategies are discussed. We provide the specific applications of Ag/CDs as FL, EC, COL, SERS, SPR, and their dual-mode sensors for sensing various chemical and biological analytes. Their detailed sensing mechanisms are also provided. Finally, the challenges and future perspectives in this area are proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and formation mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, CDs have been synthesized using several methods including hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, and reflux as mentioned in Table 1 . The hydrothermal method is an efficient and effective treatment to obtain CDs from numerous natural products and small molecules [36,37]. This method produces CDs with consistent shapes and a moderate quantum yield (QY) [38,39]. It uses a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave as a place to insert a precursor along with water. At a high temperature, a C seeding core was established and CDs were grown [40]. Like hydrothermal, the solvothermal method is the most efficient treatment and is broadly utilized to produce CDs with organic solvents. The composition and properties of CDs can be adjusted carefully by different factors in the reaction process, including the selection of precursors, solvents, reaction duration, and temperature [41]. It produced colorful CDs from blue to red utilizing molecular or polymer precursors. This method is preferred because it creates CDs with a higher QY compared to top-down approaches.

Furthermore, the microwave method has gained increased attention due to its energy efficiency, quick reaction time, and low cost [42]. A microwave is an electromagnetic wave with a wavelength ranging from 1 mm to 1 m, providing concentrated energy to break the chemical bonds in the substrate [43]. Besides, pyrolysis is a general and simple method to obtain CDs. A compound or substance decomposes chemically utilizing heat/combustion [13]. External combustion could assist in the dehydration and carbonization of the starting material and convert it into CDs particles.

Moreover, the reflux method offers an alternative approach to produce CDs. This method is notably simple, efficient, and economical, requiring minimal equipment and instruments, which suggests the potential for enabling large-scale production [44]. The precursor is combined with a solvent in a reflux system at a specific moderate temperature and duration, followed by purification to yield CDs. The size and optical characteristics can be readily adjusted using this method [45,46]. Among the mentioned synthesis methods, hydrothermal treatment is widely used to produce CDs. The C precursors can be obtained from natural products/biomasses (i.e. pulp-free orange juice and Lycii fructus) or small molecules (i.e. chitosan, glucose, citric acid, asparagine, and urea).

The CDs' synthesis is influenced by various parameters, including pH, temperature, reaction medium choice, dopant types, C precursors, and the ratio of C source to reaction medium [54]. Among these, the duration of the reaction time is critical for converting precursors into CDs and determining their optical properties. Extended hydrothermal processes have been observed to increase the QY of CDs [55]. However, excessively prolonged synthesis periods can lead to the CDs' structural breakdown due to unregulated carbonization. Additionally, CDs synthesized under microwave irradiation for <5 min showed no FL, emphasizing the need for an adequate synthesis duration for complete carbonization of the precursors [56]. Careful adjustment of the reaction time duration provides valuable insights into the formation mechanism of CDs, essential for a comprehensive understanding of the precise processes involved in achieving complete CDs formation.

A documented mechanism outlines four distinct stages in the formation of CDs through the manipulation of reaction time duration including precursor decomposition, subsequent aromatization, followed by nucleation, and ultimately the growth of CDs [57]. CDs derived from biomass sources often exhibit low FL intensity due to inherent surface defects that render them unstable. Therefore, to enhance brightness and QY, surface passivation or doping is essential and crucial [58]. The augmentation of FL through doping arises from creating new FL centers by capturing highly radiative electron-hole pairs [59]. An alternative mechanism for CDs formation utilizing microwave irradiation has been proposed [10]. According to this suggested process, the precursor initially undergoes dehydration, leading to the aggregation of decomposed precursor particles and mild condensation, forming large NPs. These NPs then reduce the size due to ongoing intramolecular dehydration during microwave irradiation. Simultaneously, CC bonds form, and aromatic functional groups emerge. When the concentrated clusters reach a critical supersaturation point, nucleation of CDs occurs. The nucleus arises through the dispersion of aromatic groups on the NPs surface, and the passivation of various functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -C=O, etc.) takes place. Over time, NPs tend to transform into CDs. In the final phase, small-sized CDs particles with narrower distributions are produced. Additionally, this formation mechanism can be used to indicate the high quality of the CDs and is therefore linked to QY and FL intensity, as CDs with a well-organized arrangement of a graphitic core exhibit exceptional FL properties.

The hydrothermal method involves the formation of a C core through the dehydration of the initial molecule or the creation of a fluorophore, with an increase in carbonization temperature or time. Generally, the QYs of FL molecules within these CDs are considerably higher than the band gap FL efficiency resulting from the Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE) of the conjugated π-domains. However, the molecular FL stability is lower compared to band gap FL [60]. The FL emission of CDs is significantly enhanced through doping with heteroatoms, and the cooperative effect of co-doping aids in the formation of band gaps, resulting in a high QY. The impact of various preparation methods or precursors on the FL mechanism of CDs remains uncertain. Methods utilizing readily available natural starting materials in specific regions may be less reproducible elsewhere. Users generally prefer methods based on accessible materials of consistent quality regardless of location or season.

When CDs are prepared with pure organic chemicals, different effects may occur. Citric acid (CA), as an example of defined organic chemicals, is presently the most commonly used precursor due to its influence on the reproducible preparation of CDs. However, under the same synthesis conditions, precursors with larger conjugate structures and intense FL activities are more advantageous for obtaining long-wavelength emissions from CDs. These precursors facilitate the self-assembly of the C core, and the various functional groups in the precursors contribute to the formation of the surface state of CDs. Additionally, the choice of solvents can influence the dehydration and carbonization of precursors, and by altering the composition of reaction solvents, the bandgaps of CDs can be adjusted, thereby affecting the emitted color [60].

The “top-down” approach involves breaking down sheet-like or bulky C materials, like carbohydrates, through chemical, electrochemical, or physical means, relying on the disruption of bonds between C atoms [61]. For green synthesis of CDs, researchers commonly employ waste C-containing materials like candle soot as the precursor. This larger C material can be oxidized and converted into nanosized CDs with strong oxidants like H2SO4 or HNO3. However, the “top-down” method is less frequently used in green synthesis due to its reliance on specific raw materials. Conversely, “bottom-up” synthesis entails the pyrolysis or carbonization of small organic molecules, typically occurring in four stages: condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [62]. Initially, small organic molecule precursors undergo condensation reactions like amidation, aldol condensation, Schiff base condensation, and radical reactions to form chain compound intermediates. These intermediates then aggregate into polymer-like CDs, also known as C clusters, through covalent, non-covalent, or other interactions. Subsequently, the polymers undergo carbonization, especially at high temperatures, to establish the C core. Finally, residual precursors act as surface passivating agents, enhancing FL efficiency. This comprehensive process encompasses precursor pyrolysis, the transition from sp3 to sp2-hybridized C, species reorganization, and more [61]. Additionally, reactions among specific functional groups, like –NH2 and –OH, play a crucial role in CDs formation, while the C conversion from sp3 to sp2 occurs during pyrolysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AgNPs and Ag/CDs Nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In general, AgNPs are typically produced through the chemical reduction of Ag precursor materials, such as AgNO3 or AgCl, using reducing agents like hydrazine, NaBH4, and citrate compounds [63–66]. In addition to this capping/protecting agent, thiol or amine-containing organic compounds are critical to avoid the aggregation of AgNPs. However, these methods involve either commercially expensive and toxic as well as consuming and tedious synthesis and purification processes. Recently, CDs have been identified with multifunction roles such as green reducing agent, protecting, and capping agent for the formation of metal NPs due to the existence of plentiful functional groups such as -C-O, -C=O, -NH2, -COO-, and -O-H presented on the CDs' surface [67,68]. However, CDs have been recognized as an environmentally sustainable and green reducing agent for the synthesis of AgNPs, following green chemistry principles. In this part, we discussed several synthesis strategies and the formation mechanism of Ag/CDs.

The preparation methods for Ag/CDs with various conditions are informed in Table 2 . The CDs and AgNPs were generally mixed, stirred, and incubated at different temperatures and times. The chemical reduction was carried out using NaOH and NaBH4. Ag/CDs are mostly prepared using the chemical reduction method where CDs as the reducing agent. Essentially, many O-containing surface functional groups like -OH, -C=O, -COOH groups of CDs can reduce Ag+ ions into Ag0 by relocating electrons [69]. The significant benefit of this reduction method is that CDs can also play a critical role as stabilizing, capping, or protecting agents during the formation of Ag/CDs. Typically, this method requires 2 to 24 h at room temperature to form Ag/CDs.

In addition, photochemical reduction was established for the production of high-quality Ag/CDs. In this method, the composition of Ag precursor and electron-donating compounds such as CDs were photoexcited with UV irradiation to generate Ag/CDs at low temperatures [71,72]. The nucleation and growth of Ag/CDs are typically attributed to electron transfer from photoexcited CDs to Ag+ ions. In this process, Ag+ ions initially interact with CDs through electrostatic attraction mediated by their surface functional groups. Upon photoexcitation, free electrons generated within the CDs are transferred to the surface of Ag+ ions, leading to their reduction to metallic Ag and the formation of Ag/CDs.

Furthermore, the use of microwave energy has been employed in the synthesis of AgNPs. This application combines the advantages of rapid reduction processes and consistent heating of metal precursors, facilitated by the localized superheating generated during the process [73,74]. During microwave irradiation, the CDs' surface functional groups interact with Ag+ ions and reduce into metallic Ag, which further avoids aggregation of NPs. This method produced Ag/CDs within 30 s to 10 min. In comparison with other methods, microwave synthesis is a simple, very fast, and eco-friendly method.

To further advance the fabrication of Ag/CDs, numerous specific strategies and methods are available through synthetic and physical approaches. Firstly, precision control over the size, shape, and distribution of AgNPs can be achieved using seed-mediated growth or template-directed techniques, influencing the synthesis of both AgNPs and CDs [75]. Secondly, enhancing stability and compatibility with target analytes can be achieved through surface modifications on AgNPs and CDs, involving the introduction of ligands, polymers, or functional groups [76]. Thirdly, the establishment of a core-shell structure, where AgNPs serve as the core and CDs form the outer shell, provides additional protection and stability to the AgNPs [77]. Fourth, altering the electronic properties of AgNPs by introducing dopants can enhance their performance in specific applications [78]. Fifth, combining Ag/CDs with nanomaterials such as graphene or metal oxides can yield hybrid composites with synergistic properties, including improved conductivity and sensing capabilities [79]. Sixth, templates like mesoporous materials or biomolecules can guide the growth and arrangement of AgNPs and CDs, offering enhanced structural control control [80]. Seventh, integrating the synthesis of AgNPs and CDs directly onto the sensor substrate or matrix ensures a robust and stable interaction between the sensing elements and the substrate [81]. Eighth, applying a protective layer or coating (e.g., polymer, silica) on the surface of AgNPs and CDs can prevent aggregation, enhance stability, and reduce potential interactions with interfering substances [82]. Ninth, post-synthesis treatments like annealing, chemical etching, or surface activation can refine the structural and surface properties of Ag/CDs [83]. Finally, comprehensive characterization of Ag/CDs using several techniques such as TEM, SEM, XRD, and spectroscopy allows for a thorough understanding of their morphology, crystal structure, and optical properties, facilitating fine-tuning of the fabrication process [84]. The utilization of these strategies and methods significantly enhances the fabrication of Ag/CDs, resulting in sensors with improved sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and performance for sensing applications.

UV–vis spectroscopy employing UV and visible range radiation is a standard and commonly available spectroscopic technique for measuring the optical properties of Ag/CDs. As reported by Walekar et al. [42], the UV–vis spectra of Ag/N-CDs exhibited a modest wide peak at 277 nm and a sharp peak at 408 nm, which correspond to the N-CDs and AgNPs absorbances, respectively (Fig. 2A). In the case of Ag colloids, the SPR band at 408 nm was stable and symmetric in shape [43]. Liu et al. [44], also measured the UV–vis absorption spectra of polyethyleneimine (PEI), CDs, and Ag/CDs (Fig. 2B). The n → π* transition originates from the CO bonds with an adsorption band at around 360 nm. However, the -NH- bonds can encourage the red-shift of this band [45]. Upon the addition of Ag+ ions, a new peak at 394 nm formed, corresponding to the physical absorption of AgNPs [85]. Jana and colleagues evaluated the absorbance of AgNPs and CDs solution, where a peak at 390 nm for AgNPs and the absence of a distinct peak for the CDs [70], as shown in Fig. 2C. Also, a broad absorption with a peak at nearly 371 nm was observed in the UV–Vis spectrum of Ag/CDs [86], indicating the creation of AgNPs (Fig. 2D). In addition, CDs and Ag/CDs have also been studied by Beiraghi and coworkers where the peak located at 340 nm of CDs can be attributed to carbonyl and amino functional groups of CDs (Fig. 2E) [34]. When AgNPs were used in conjunction with CDs, an absorption band could be identified at 430 nm, however, when AgNPs were used alone, an absorption band could be noticed at 390 nm. This red-shift indicated that the CDs had been properly linked to AgNPs. The broadening of the peak in Ag/CDs can also be attributed to the increase of AgNPs' size due to their interaction with CDs.

Furthermore, FL spectroscopy is a routine and widely accessible spectroscopic technique for measuring the optical properties of Ag/CDs. Jin et al. [89] investigated the excitation-dependent FL properties of CDs and Ag/CDs. High FL intensity of CDs was obtained with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 370 nm and 445 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). The FL of CDs was quenched after introducing AgNPs to form Ag/CDs. There is a possibility that this result is due to an efficient electron transfer process from CDs to AgNPs, which can be related to the annihilation of the non-radiative electron/hole recombination in the CDs. Given that radiative recombination of excitons is a significant contributor to the FL properties of CDs. In another work, the FL is also reduced when AgNPs are in the NS-CDs' solution (Fig. 3B) [70]. Amjadi and colleagues [90] found that the FL intensity of CDs decreased significantly as the concentration of AgNPs increased, revealing a highly efficient quenching process (Fig. 3C). Zhang et al. [90] measured the emission spectrum of Ag/N-CDs excited at 370 nm. The distinct peaks at 450 nm and 650 nm were observed (Fig. 3D), indicating that using Ag NCs and N-CDs as a ratiometric nanoprobe was feasible. The FL intensity at 650 nm increased with increasing Ag NCs' concentrations from 0 to 0.28 mg/mL while remaining slightly reduced above this concentration due to FL self-quenching. The FL intensity of N-CDs was gradually quenched by increasing the concentration of Ag NCs. In addition, CDs with excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 520 nm have FL emission spectra in the visible light region [91]. On the other hand, Ag/CDs nanohybrids with excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 620 nm exhibited prominent FL in the near-infrared (NIR) region (Fig. 3E).

Raman spectroscopy is one of the most frequently used non-destructive and non-invasive spectroscopic techniques for CDs characterization. Jin et al. reported the Raman spectra of CDs and core-shell structured Ag/CDs, with the spectrum of CDs exhibiting no discernible Raman signal, likely attributed to their high FL properties [89]. However, the Raman spectrum of Ag/CDs showed clear peaks of C species at 1600 cm-1 (G band) and 1350 cm-1 (D band), indicating the presence of C species (Fig. 4A). This result demonstrated that the formation of Ag/CDs significantly reduced the strong FL of CDs and considerably increased the ability to obtain an obvious Raman signal for CDs. Amjadi and colleagues [51] reported the Raman spectrum of Ag/CDs, revealing two peaks at 1358 cm-1 and 1590 cm-1, characteristic of the D and G bands in C nanomaterials (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, Yang et al. [93] reported the Raman spectra of Ag/CDs and CDs at an excitation wavelength of 633 nm (Fig. 4C). It is evident from the ranges that there are two prominent peaks located at 1360 cm-1 and 1540 cm-1, which correspond to the previously documented D and G bands, respectively [94]. The D band is associated with vibrations of sp3 C atoms with dangling bonds in the plane terminations of disordered or defected graphene or glassy carbons. The hexagonal lattice vibrations of sp2-bonded C atoms are associated with the G band, which corresponds to the E2g mode of graphene. ID/IG is a ratio used to determine the extent of disorder in C materials and the percentage of sp3 to sp2 C. Ag/CDs (red line) have an ID/IG ratio significantly higher than that of CDs due to the SERS effect of AgNPs on the surface of the CDs.

Because of its low cost, rapidity, and ease of sample preparation, FTIR spectroscopy is a widely used tool for characterizing the functional groups on the surface of CDs and Ag/CDs. The aspects of the CDs surface that have reduced activity by hydroxyl groups are evident in the FTIR spectrum. As a result, CDs can act as electron donors by donating electrons to the electron-withdrawing agents. The FTIR spectrum confirms the presence of -COOH groups on the surface of CDs, which have a strong affinity for ag+ ions [51]. in this case, -cooh groups on the surface of cds are likely responsible for the absorption of Ag+ ions. Liu et al. used FTIR spectroscopy to evaluate the characteristic peaks of Ag/CDs (Fig. 5A) [88]. The FTIR spectrum of CDs (Spectrum B) represents a new peak at 1637 cm-1 which can be assigned to the amide group (-CONH-), and the peak at 3362 cm-1 is associated with the secondary amino group [88]. Three peaks at 1474, 1314, and 1110 cm-1 are assigned to the bending and stretching vibrations of OH, respectively [95]. Moreover, the peak at 2838 cm−1 may be attributed to the vibration of C − H from the aldehyde group. When the Ag/CDs were synthesized (Spectrum C), the intensities of the bands for the secondary amino group at 3426 cm−1 and the −CONH− group at 1638 cm−1 decreased, providing a signal for the formation of the Ag/CDs. Additionally, the absorption band of the OH groups almost disappeared whereas a strong band at 1381 cm−1 for C − H bending appeared, which may belong to the oxidized reducing aldehyde group [96].

Moreover, Dong et al. discovered that CDs contain a large number of -COOH and -OH groups (Fig. 5B-a) [86], which can form bonds with Ag+ ions through ionic or coordination bondings [94]. CDs-Ag+ complex can be developed due to the adsorbed Ag+ ions on the surface of the CDs. Ag+ ions are reduced into AgNPs during the subsequent chemical reduction with NaBH4, while many O-containing groups are removed from the CDs (Fig. 5B-b). Many CDs' particles are deposited on the surfaces of AgNPs, forming Ag/CDs. FTIR spectrum of Ag/CDs showed primarily absorption peaks of OH at 3400 cm-1 and 3300 cm-1, CO at 1720 cm-1, CC at 1620 cm-1, and CC at 3400 cm-1. Ag/CDs were found to contain graphitic C structures with abundant -COOH and -OH groups. Because of the presence of these functional groups, the Ag/CDs exhibited excellent dispersion and long-term stability in water. When the FTIR spectrum of Ag/CDs was compared to the spectrum of CDs, it can be seen that the relative absorption intensity of the CO group to the CC group in Ag/CDs decreased significantly, as shown in Fig. 5B. This result indicates that the O-containing groups on the CDs were partially removed during the functionalization with AgNPs. Furthermore, Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz investigated the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds attributed to the broad peak that appeared at 3277 cm-1 [34,97]. According to the results, the stretching vibrations of CH, CO, and CN bonds, could be assigned to the peaks centered at 2944, 1705, and 1663 cm-1, respectively. It has been determined that the stretching vibrations of CC are responsible for the band at 1566 cm-1. In contrast, the peaks at 1434, 1399, 1285, and 1183 cm-1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of COO-, CN, aromatic C-NH, and CO, respectively [98]. The results revealed that the surface of CDs contained many functional groups, which are well-suited for effective interaction with metal ions (Fig. 5C-a). In addition, the FTIR spectrum of the Ag/CDs (Fig. 5C-b) demonstrated that the synthesis of the Ag/CDs was successful, with absorption bands at 1228, 1431, 1548, 1653, 2938, and 3282 cm-1 corresponding to CO, COO-, CC, CO, NH, and OH bonds, respectively. As a result, AgNPs modified with CDs containing many COO-, –OH, and –NH2 groups may interact with metal ions.

Shen and colleagues also measured the FTIR spectra of CDs and Ag/CDs [47]. Before reduction, the broad -OH stretching vibration is observed at 3390 cm-1 along with absorption at approximately 1000 cm-1, indicating the presence of -OH in the CDs (Fig. 5D). CDs exhibit absorption peaks at 3390 and 1660 cm-1, attributed to asymmetric stretching and deformational vibration of NH2, respectively. This indicates that the surface of CDs contains an abundance of -NH2 groups. The stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring at 1564, 1498, 1440, and 1369 cm-1 are attributed to the CC stretching vibrations, and the out-of-plane bending vibration of the = CH groups of the aromatic ring is attributed to the 865 and 686 cm-1 bands. At 898 cm-1, the CDs exhibit typical absorptions of CCH wagging vibrations.

Extraction of surface chemical information from a material using the XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) provides information about the surface properties and electronic state of the elements within the material. According to Amjadi et al., the XPS spectrum of Ag/CDs exhibited peaks for the C1s, Ag 3d, and O1s elements at 284, 367, and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 6A-a) [51]. The presence of CO, CC, and CO groups in the Ag/CDs was demonstrated by the high-resolution XPS of C1s (Fig. 6A-c). Aside from that, the high-energy XPS peaks observed at 371 and 365 eV can be attributed to the binding energies of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively (Fig. 6A-b). These results confirm that AgNPs are present on the surface of CDs and that the decoration is made of Ag. XPS results showed that the C and Ag contents in the Ag/CDs were found to be 57% and 0.26% respectively. Liu et al. reported the XPS spectrum which shows that CDs contain C, N, and O elements, and the contents of the three elements were calculated to be 67.85%, 30.15%, and 2%, respectively (Fig. 6B-a) [88]. The C1s scan spectrum could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.5 eV (CC) and 285.5 eV (CN), respectively [99]. The N1s spectrum of CDs can be fitted into two peaks at 398.3 and 399.3 eV, which are assigned to N in a graphite-like structure and the O=C–N group, respectively [100]. The C1s and N1s spectra demonstrate that amide groups are formed within CDs. The two fitted peaks at 531 and 532.6 eV from the O1s spectrum are described as CO and C–OH/C–O C groups, respectively [36]. As shown in Fig. 6B-b, compared to the XPS spectrum of CDs, the XPS full scan spectrum of the Ag/CDs exhibits a new peak of the Ag element, indicating that the Ag element has been introduced. Furthermore, the intensity of O element was raised, whereas the intensity of N element was lowered when comparing the C1s spectrum to the CDs spectrum, a new peak at 288 eV can be fitted, attributed to the CO groups [36]. Following this characteristic, the CN bond of CDs was disrupted during the formation of the Ag/CDs, resulting in more CO groups. The three-dimensional (3d) spectrum of Ag reveals two peaks with binding energies of 367.6 and 373.7 eV for 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively, indicating the Ag metallic nature [96].

The XPS spectrum of the Ag/CDs [86], exhibits a peak of C1s at approximately 284 eV and a peak of O1s at around 531 eV (Fig. 6C-a), as well as four peaks of Ag3d5/2, Ag3d3/2, Ag3p3/2, and Ag3p1/2 at 367, 373, 571, and 604 eV, respectively [101]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C1s peak (Fig. 6C-b) reveals three prominent peaks associated with C atoms, which are located at 284.6 eV (CC sp2), 286.3 eV (CO), and 288.3 eV (C=O), respectively. Jin et al. used XPS to analyze the chemical structure of the Ag/CDs [89]. A full-range XPS spectrum displayed the typical signals of C, O, N, and Ag for the Ag/CDs (Fig. 6D-a). Fig. 6D-b shows the main peaks of C atoms in the Ag/CDs at 284.7, 285.7, 286.5, and 287.7 eV, attributed to C-C/C=C, CN, CO, and O=C-O-Ag groups, respectively. Using the main peaks of O1s as shown in Fig. 6D-c, it is possible to fit them with two prominent peaks at 531.7 and 532.6 eV, which were assigned to the elements C and O, respectively. In part, the CO groups were formed due to the oxidation of -OH groups on the CDs, which resulted in the formation of AgNPs by inducing the growth of AgNPs. When looking at the N1s fine spectrum in Fig. 6D-d, it is possible to attribute the peaks observed at 398.5, 399.5, and 400.6 eV to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N groups, respectively. Fig. 6D-e shows that the Ag3d peaks have two doublet components; the low binding energy signals at 367.8 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.8 eV (Ag 3d3/2) were associated with the metallic Ag, while another two peaks at 369.1 and 375.1 eV were associated with the O=C-O-Ag.

Nanomaterials can be studied using the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) instrument, which provides information on their particle size, size distribution, and surface morphology. Fig. 7 reveals the spherical Ag/CDs ranging from 14 ± 3 nm in size. Furthermore, the HR-TEM images reveal that the interplanar spacings for two clear lattice fringes were 0.23 nm and 0.20 nm, respectively, and that they correspond to the crystal structure of (111) and (200) Ag lattice planes, respectively (Fig. 7A) [51]. Jana et al. obtained a TEM image in which AgNPs were approximately 7-8 nm in size and anchored to N,S-CDs as shown in Fig. 7B-a [47]. As a result, the N,S-CDs were mostly eclipsed and formed a conjugate within a diameter range of 4-5 nm, and the AgNPs were within a diameter range of 7-8 nm. The HRTEM image presented two distinct types of lattice fringes, with 0.26 nm for C and 0.29 nm and 0.21 nm for metallic Ag being the smallest and largest ones, respectively (Fig. 7B-b). Dong et al. obtained a TEM image (Fig. 7C-a) where the Ag/CDs were approximately 20-30 nm in size and lacked regular morphology [86]. Using HRTEM, a thin layer of CDs thickness of roughly 1 to 2 nm as shown in Fig. 7C-b. In addition, Beiraghi and Najibi-Gehraz determined the size and shape of the AgNPs and Ag/CDs using TEM [34]. The AgNPs were spherical and well-dispersed, with a particle size of approximately 7 nm (Fig. 7D-a), whereas the average diameter of the Ag/CDs was about 20 nm (Fig. 7D-b). Fortunately, the AgNPs retained their uniformity and good distribution after being modified with CDs. Shen et al. discovered that the average size of the CDs was 29.4 and 6.9 nm (Fig. 7E-a) [47]. Fig. 7E-b shows that after heating the AgNO3-CDs mixture, TEM images validated the presence of well-dispersed AgNPs. It should be noted that the CDs were barely visible in the image due to the low contrast between the dots and the used substrate. The diameters of AgNPs ranged from 2 to 4 nm, with an average diameter of 3.1-1.5 nm. The HRTEM image also revealed high crystallinity of the AgNPs, as indicated by a well-defined crystal lattice of 0.244 nm (inset Fig. 7E-b), which was attributed to Ag (111). Table 3 shows several properties of Ag/CDs including the FL excitation/emission wavelengths, absorbances, size distributions, and average sizes. The FL properties generally derived from CDs due to the addition of AgNPs lowered the intensity of emission spectra. Meanwhile, the absorbance peaks are from AgNPs at around 400 nm.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a rapid analytical technique used to identify and characterize the phases of crystalline materials based on their diffraction patterns. Liu et al. investigated the XRD patterns of Ag/CDs. Fig. 8A shows that CDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak centered on 23° (Fig. 8A-a), attributed to the highly disordered C atoms [88]. In the case of the Ag/CDs (Fig. 8A-b), the characteristic peaks of crystalline AgNPs are observed at 2θ = 38, 44, 64, and 78, respectively, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) lattice planes respectively [102,103]. The peak at 23° corresponding to CDs was notably diminished upon the formation of Ag/CDs, indicating the removal of disordered C atoms in the CDs upon their attachment to the surface of AgNPs, as reported earlier. Amjadi and colleagues also discovered the XRD pattern of Ag/CDs [51]. It has been discovered that the peaks observed at 2θ = 38, 44, 65, and 78° are characteristic of the crystalline (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of Ag, respectively (Fig. 8B). The formation of crystalline AgNPs on the surface of CDs is responsible for the appearance of these peaks. Furthermore, the typical (002) peak of CDs can be observed around 2θ = 17, corresponding to the formation of AgNPs on the surface of the CDs. The intensity of this peak is very weak and difficult to distinguish. On the other hand, the XRD pattern of CDs exhibits a broad (002) peak around 19°, but the shift angle is lower. This phenomenon may be caused by an increase in the interlayer spacing of CDs due to the crystallization of AgNPs [102–104].

When Jin et al. investigated the chemical structure of prepared core-shell nanostructured Ag/CDs by XRD [89]. Fig. 8C depicts the XRD patterns of Ag/CDs, which have a low C-lattice structure content due to CDs in the sample [105]. As a result, the characteristic peak for C at 25° was so weak that it could not be detected, and the pattern only showed the characteristic peaks for Ag. This was confirmed by the observation of four distinct diffraction peaks at 38° (111), 44o (200), 64° (220), and 78o (311) planes, which were attributed to planes of the face-centered cubic lattice of Ag, respectively, indicating a good crystallization of AgNPs. However, two additional peaks were observed at 33.5o and 55.7o. The peak at 33.5o was particularly intense, with the intensity peaking at 33.5o being particularly strong. It was discovered that the two peaks were similar to the cubic phase Ag2O 32.9 (111) and 54.9 (220) diffraction planes. However, their positions differed from each other, and the other two (200) and (311) cubic phase diffraction peaks were not observed.

Furthermore, Jana and colleagues discovered that the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 38.3, 44.4, 64.7, 77.7, and 81.75 correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes of AgNPs, respectively (Fig. 8D) [70]. Besides, the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy technique was used by Yang et al. to investigate the chemical composition of Ag/CDs [93]. In Fig. 8E (red line), Ag/CDs are composed of only C, O, and Ag elements, with no other impurities, whereas the Cu signal is thought to originate from the Cu grid [55]. On the right side of Fig. 8E (black line), we can see the XRD patterns of Ag/CDs, which depict the characteristic diffraction peaks of Ag and C. Four peaks with 2θ = 38.1, 44.3, 64.4, and 77.4 are attributed to diffractions of Ag at (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively. Moreover, a broad peak at 2θ = 26 is attributed to (002) diffractions of graphitic C, demonstrating the formation of Ag/CDs.

Ag/CDs have been used extensively and are still being developed for use in various chemical and biological sensing applications including FL, EC, COL, SERS, and SPR sensors. Some Ag/CDs also functioned as dual-mode sensor types. These sensors are used to detect multiple biomolecules, toxic materials, environmental pollutants, and other essential analytes. Various prepared Ag/CDs used for chemical and biological sensing applications are listed in Table 4 . Most Ag/CDs could detect multiple analytes with outstanding performances at low levels (<1 μM). Ag/CDs are still prepared to improve their performances for many unique applications.

FL sensors have been used in various applications for many years and have several advantages, including high specificity, high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, low detection limits, and ease of operation [135,136]. CDs have several benefits, including increased water solubility, bright FL in the visible light spectrum, low cost, low toxicity, good optical properties, high photostability, good biocompatibility, and capability for rapid and straightforward functionalization [137–139]. While AgNPs have a high sensitivity, high photostability, and low toxicity, they are also highly reactive due to their SPR property [140,141]. CDs are effective FL quenchers resulting from the energy transfer process between AgNPs and CDs [142,143]. As a result, the combination of AgNPs and CDs exhibits superiority and can be used as an FL sensor to detect a wide range of biomolecules and potentially hazardous materials in the environment.

Several papers reported the utilization of Ag/CDs as FL sensors to detect Ag2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, S2-, sulfite/bisulfite, 6-thioguanine (6-TG), ascorbic acid (AA), Cys, adenosine, cysteamine, bacteria, uric acid, glyphosate, tetracycline, gemcitabine, glucose, metformin hydrochloride, and cephalexin. For instance, Amjadi and colleagues used Ag/CDs as an FL sensor to detect 6-thioguanine (6-TG) in human plasma samples [92]. The relationship between FL intensity enhancement and 6-TG concentrations (0.03-1 μM) was linear, with the limit of detection (LOD) at 0.01 μM. When 6-TG was added to the CDs solution, there was no change in the FL intensity. When CDs were mixed with AgNPs, the FL was quenched. The FL intensities of Ag/CDs gradually increased upon introducing 6-TG into the Ag/CDs system. The formation of AgS bonds between 6-TG and the Ag/CDs led to the aggregation of AgNPs. This interaction mitigated the quenching effect, resulting in heightened emission intensity. The sensing mechanism for this analyte is based on energy transfer between them.

Tian et al. fabricated Ag/CDs with enhanced near-infrared FL by metal-enhanced FL (MEF) [91]. It was a simple method and did not require an extra reductant. The Ag/CDs were used as a probe to identify AA in urine and human serum samples. The LOD for AA in a linear range of 0.2 to 60 μM was obtained to be 25 nM. The FL of the Ag-CDs was quenched by Fe3+ through an electron transfer effect. When Fe3+ is introduced to the Ag-CDs in the presence of AA, the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA occurs before the electron transfer between Fe3+ and the Ag-CDs, resulting in no alteration in the FL of the Ag-CDs. The sensing mechanism was based on the competition between Ag/CDs and AA to Fe3+. In addition, Mu et al. reported the creation of an “on-off” FL probe based on Ag/CDs for the effective and precise detection of cysteamine in bovine serum samples [144]. As the concentration of cysteamine grew, the CDs' FL emission intensity gradually increased (Fig. 9A). Over the concentration range of 2-16 μM, LOD of 0.35 μM was attained (Fig. 9B). Photographs of the samples taken under a UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 9C) make it abundantly evident that water-soluble AgNPs were responsible for the CDs' altered FL. The probe was used to detect cysteamine in the presence of thirteen distinct interferences, including Ala, Gly, Glu, Met, Lys, Leu, Trp, Ser, His, Hcy, Cys, GSH, and sodium sulfide, to assess the selectivity of Ag/CDs as shown in Fig. 9D. The FL of CDs was notably diminished when AgNPs were combined with the CDs solution, primarily due to the inner filter effect (IFE). Upon introducing cysteamine into the aqueous mixture of CDs and AgNPs, the formation of AgS bonds between cysteamine and AgNPs led to the agglomeration of AgNPs. This, in turn, reinstated the quenching of CDs' FL (Fig. 9E). Based on the experimental results, the Ag/CDs were more highly selective for cysteamine than other amino acids.

Recently, Zhang et al. prepared Ag/CDs to detect the Hg2+ ions by the turn-on mechanism [145]. The FL probe has an excellent selectivity towards Hg2+ over other metal ions. Good linearity was established over the concentration of 100-160 μM and the LOD for Hg2+ was calculated to be 2.22 × 10-8 M. The presence of AgNPs in the CDs solution leads to the quenching of CD's FL through the IFE. This is because Hg2+ ions exhibit a stronger affinity for AgNPs compared to the affinity between AgNPs and CDs. Consequently, the AgNPs are displaced from the Ag/CDs, causing the FL of CDs to be restored. On the other hand, Ag nanoclusters (NCs)/N-CDs, synthesized through IFE, were utilized for the ratiometric and visual detection of Cu2+ [117]. Upon the addition of Cu2+, the red FL was quenched due to a robust chelating interaction between glutathione (GSH) from AgNCs and Cu2+, while the blue FL was restored as a consequence of the weakening of the IFE. The probe exhibited selectivity for Cu2+ and demonstrated a linear correlation in the concentration range of 0–60 μM Cu2+, with an LOD of 0.13 μM. Upon the introduction of Cu2+ to AgNCs/N-CDs, Cu2+ interacted separately with the AgNCs and N-CDs. In the sensing mechanism, the FL of AgNCs was quenched, and the FL of CDs was revived upon the addition of Cu2+ to AgNCs/N-CDs. This suggested that Cu2+ could form a non-FL complex with AgNCs and subsequently quench the FL of AgNCs. The chelating ability of GSH to Cu2+ was strong, as Cu2+ could form a stable tetragonal Cu2+-GSH complex by chelating with the -COOH and -NH2 groups on the surface of AgNCs/N-CDs. This complex blocked the charge transfer of ligands to Ag, induced the aggregation of AgNCs/N-CDs, and consequently led to the FL quenching of AgNCs/N-CDs.

Several other studies have explored using FL sensors to detect various (bio)molecules. For instance, Amjadi et al. investigated the application of Ag/CDs in detecting cysteine (Cys) in human plasma and urine samples and achieved an LOD of 4 nM [33]. In their sensing mechanism, the presence of AgNPs led to a significant decrease in the FL intensity of CDs, attributed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Upon introducing Cys to the Ag/CDs mixture, Cys molecules interacted with AgNPs via AgS bonds. This interaction prevented CDs from interacting with AgNPs, resulting in the high aggregation of NPs due to Cys absorption onto the surface of AgNPs. The reduced FRET efficiency in the Ag/CDs system happened, ultimately restoring the FL of CDs. Besides, the detection of Hg2+ ions employed the FRET process, utilizing the interaction between CDs and AgNPs [113]. The calibration plot exhibited linearity in the concentration range of 0.5–500 nmol L−1 with LOD of 0.10 nmol/L. The FRET system involved AgNPs as the desired acceptor, capable of absorbing non-radiative energy from excited CDs and reacting with Hg2+ ions through a redox system. In contrast to bulk Ag, where the standard reduction potential (E°) matches that of Hg2+ ions, the E° of AgNPs is lower due to their altered size. CDs functioned as the donor, and AgNPs as the acceptor, leading to FL quenching of the CDs in the FRET process. In the presence of Hg2+ ions, the FL emission of the Ag/CDs system was restored, as AgNPs underwent oxidation by Hg2+ ions, resulting in a turn-on FL intensity directly proportional to the Hg2+ ion concentration. This innovative combination of the FRET system with the redox reaction was employed to construct a turn-off/on FL sensor for quantifying Hg2+ ions. Additionally, Yousefi and Saraji developed Ag/N,S-CDs for determining adenosine (AD) in human urine samples, achieving an LOD of 13 nM [50]. Their sensing mechanism used a mixture of N,S-CDs and AgNPs for aptasensing. The binding of AD to the anti-AD aptamer (AP) provided greater protection against salt-induced aggregation for unmodified AgNPs than AP alone. Based on the IFE, the FL signal of N,S-CDs was quenched by the dispersed AgNPs. In the absence of AD, AgNPs tended to aggregate in a high-salt solution. Consequently, the FL spectrum of N,S-CDs no longer overlapped with the absorption spectrum of the aggregated AgNPs, and as a result, N,S-CDs did not experience quenching.

Moreover, a simple detection for glucose based on Ag/GDs/CS-modified glassy C electrodes was reported by Tran et al. [120]. They applied this nanocomposite in a glucose oxidase-based glucose sensor, by drop-casting a mixture of Ag/GDs and chitosan (CS) on GCE. This sensor exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, a linear dependence on glucose concentration in the range of 0.1–10 mM, and an LOD of 0.01 mM. In the sensing mechanism, Ag/GDs acted as a suitable catalyst for the electroreduction of H2O2 generated from glucose reaction with glucose oxidase and O2. Apart from the practical demonstration of Ag/C NC, theoretical studies to detect trinitrotoluene (TNT) and p-nitrophenol (p-NP) by EC sensors were also studied by Jadoon et al. [148]. GDs were used as a reductant. In terms of the sensing mechanism, analytes formed stable AgO anchoring bonds with Ag/C NC through charge transfer interactions. The interaction of TNT and p-NP with Ag/C NC significantly reduced the H-Lgap, ranging from 2.33 to 3.62 eV. This led to a notable overlap between the HOMO and LUMO, resulting in a decrease in excitation energy from 4.69 to 2.64 eV in Ag/C NC. Overall, the EC sensors are sensitive and can trace out TNT and p-p-NP in real-time use.

It is worth noting that COL sensors are very important because of their ideal characteristics in the rapid in situ detection of an analyte without additional instruments. It is possible to use COL sensors to detect a variety of analytes by displaying color changes that are visible to the naked eye [149]. These sensors have many advantages, including their simplicity, quick response time, and ease of visual detection [150]. Ag/CDs have been used as a COL sensor to detect a variety of chemical and biological samples including Cu2+, Hg2+, S2-, H2O2, methimazole, phoxim, and glucose. Amjadi et al. used Ag/CDs to detect methimazole in urine samples [51]. The unique interaction between Ag/CDs and methimazole resulted in a rapid change in the solution color from yellow to red (Fig. 11A). With the addition of methimazole, the UV–Vis peak intensity of the Ag/CDs decreased dramatically, and a peak was observed in a longer wavelength region. The calibration curve was linear over the methimazole's concentration range of 2-40 μg/L, with an LOD of 1 μg/L. In terms of the sensing mechanism, the interaction of methimazole with Ag atoms on the NPs' surface, facilitated by the AgS bond, could potentially induce destabilization and lead to the aggregation of certain small NPs, resulting in the formation of larger Ag/CDs. Zheng and colleagues utilized Ag/CDs in a COL assay to detect phoxim [48]. Upon the addition of varying concentrations of phoxim (0-100 μM), the Ag/CDs underwent aggregation, causing a color shift from yellow to red. This change was visually perceptible, as illustrated in Fig. 11B. The alteration in color was attributed to the red-shift of the SPR peak from 400 nm to 525 nm. The absorbance ratio (A525/A400) exhibited a linear relationship within 0.1-100 μM range of phoxim with LOD of 0.04 μM. The sensing mechanism for phoxim involved the interaction of numerous -NH2 and -OH/-COOH groups present in the CDs with phoxim. Specifically, the -NH2 groups adhered to the surface of AgNPs, while free -NH2, -OH, and -COOH functional groups provided stability to the Ag/CDs through repulsive electrostatic forces. Phoxim, containing cyano and ethoxy groups, induced negative charges on the Ag/CDs in acidic conditions due to the pKa of -NH2 and -COOH on the CDs surface. Simultaneously, the phoxim groups (pKa = 4.39) acquired positive charges, promoting interaction with free amino and carboxylate groups to form a coordination complex via electrostatic interactions. Consequently, the cross-linking of Ag/CDs by phoxim led to aggregation and a color transition from yellow to red, accompanied by a red-shift in the absorption peak. Besides that, Beiraghi and Najibi used Ag/CDs as a colorimetric sensor to detect Cu2+ ions [34]. This sensor was designed to detect AgNPs' aggregation and the solution color change from orange to red-brown (Fig. 11C). The absorption peak at 430 nm decreased gradually as Cu2+ concentrations increased from 0.3 to 8 μM (Fig. 11D). In contrast, the absorption peak at 525 nm increased dramatically when the LOD was reduced to 0.037 μM. The interaction occurs through electrostatic attractions between Cu2+ ions and the -COOH and -NH2 groups, serving as the sensing mechanism [151,152].

In a separate study, Kaur et al. utilized Ag/CDs to detect Hg2+ [125]. The -NH2 groups bind to NP surfaces, while –OH and –COOH groups stabilize Ag/CDs in water through repulsive electrostatic forces. The solution color changed from reddish-brown to light brown after adding Hg2+ solution to the Ag/CDs solution. Gradually, the color diminished, ultimately becoming colorless after a certain amount of Hg2+ was added. The LOD for this detection was 23.47 ppm. This phenomenon likely arises from a redox reaction between zero-valent Ag and Hg2+ ions. Upon subsequent Hg2+ addition, Hg2+ likely binds to the Ag surface, displacing the biological stabilizer entities and enabling the redox reaction.

SERS sensors can be used as a susceptible technique for the detection and identification of various (bio)molecules [153–155]. The analytical ability of a composite is determined by the signal strength of the radiation emitted due to its interaction with targeted analytes. Several composites produced Raman signals with extremely weak. Adding metal with a high atomic number can enhance the signal, referred to as SERS. As a SERS substrate, Ag/CDs with core-shell nanostructures can enhance the Raman signal of an analyte. Several reports informed the use of Ag/CDs as SERS sensors towards R6G, P. aeruginosa, H2O2, atrazine, thiram, and melamine. Jin and coworkers could directly monitor the oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 in their experiment [89]. Using Ag/CDs as both SERS substrate and catalyst, the SERS signal from the oxidized TMB product was obtained directly from the Ag/CDs. It was discovered that the bands from oxidized TMB have wavelengths of 1190, 1336, and 1605 cm-1, which correspond to the bending vibrations of CH3, CC stretching, CH bending, and aromatic ring stretching modes, respectively. SERS intensity from these bands increased gradually over time during the peroxidase process, and no other bands were observed during this period. When the bands were examined after 20 min, their intensities showed almost no change, indicating that all TMB molecules had been oxidized. Increasing the H2O2 concentrations enhanced the SERS intensities from these bands (Fig. 12A). The band at 1605 cm-1 intensity increased linearly with concentration from 3 × 10-8 M to 3 × 10-3 M, with an LOD of 1.6 × 10-8 M. The TMB oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of Ag/CDs showed enhanced SERS signals. The catalytic reaction mechanism likely arises from an improvement in the electron mobility and density of Ag/CDs NPs, facilitated by the electron transfer from the lone-pair electrons in the TMB amino groups to the CDs. The CDs serve as photosensitizing electron donors, effectively participating in photoinduced electron transfer. Some CDs promptly accelerate the electron transfer from CDs to H2O2, subsequently catalyzing the decomposition of H2O2 into OH radicals. Therefore, an improved catalytic activity for the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 is reached for Ag/CDs compared with that of CDs and AgNPs alone owing to the synergistic effect.

Additionally, Bhunia et al. created SERS-active flexible polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) films that were attached to Ag/CDs and used as a sensitive SERS sensing platform, to detect P. aeruginosa bacterial cells [35]. PDMS is a flexible and transparent polymer matrix supporting various molecular species and nanocomposites [35,156,157]. Strong vibrations were detected in the SERS spectrum of bacterial proteins at 1400 and 930 cm-1, which were associated with carboxylate stretching and S-S/C-S from amino acids containing S in the bacterial proteins (Fig. 12B) [158,159]. The interaction between CDs and AgNPs on PDMS films served as a new SERS platform, with the signal being directly correlated to the interaction between the CDs and AgNPs on the film. This SERS film was used to determine the LOD for P. aeruginosa, which was 104 cells/mL. As the sensing mechanism responsible for the Raman signals, these results denoted energy transfer from the CDs to the AgNPs.

Tang and coworkers introduced a SERS method for the selective detection of trace amounts of atrazine using R6G as a Raman reporter, which was adsorbed on Ag/CDs [127]. The signals of R6G were distinctly identifiable at a concentration of 1 × 10−7 M, while lower concentrations did not yield discernible signals. Thus, a concentration of 1 × 10−7 M R6G was employed for atrazine detection. The SERS responses of R6G at various Raman shifts increased with rising atrazine concentration, whereas the dilution of Ag/CDs resulted in diminished responses. At lower concentrations of the aggregating agent, strong repulsion due to Coulomb interaction predominantly influenced the potential energy, leading to lower aggregation probabilities, thus limiting the sensitivity of the proposed method. As the concentration of atrazine exceeded 1 μM, the SERS responses of R6G decreased. In this scenario, an increased number of atrazine molecules were bound around the surface of Ag/CDs, resulting in fewer binding sites for linking the Ag/CDs. A positive linear relationship was observed between the enhancement times and the logarithm of atrazine concentration within 10 to 1000 nM. At a Raman shift of 1509.4 cm−1, the correlation coefficient was 0.9940 and the LOD was estimated to be 5 nM. Regarding the sensing mechanism, in the presence of atrazine, the aggregation of the modified CDs occurred due to the interaction between AgNPs and atrazine, resulting in a significant enhancement of the SERS signal of R6G. The SERS signals of R6G increased with the escalating concentration of atrazine.

In addition, an innovative SERS platform specifically tailored for the detection of melamine in milk was developed [129]. This hydrophobic SERS platform was engineered through a one-step, in-situ carbonization process, involving the direct growth of hydrophobic Ag/CDs on glass. The SERS platform displayed a linear response within the concentration range of 0.01 to 100 ppm, boasting an LOD of 9 ppb for melamine detection in milk through direct application onto the platform. The enhancement of SERS signals for target molecules was ascribed to the electromagnetic enhancement mechanism, where the optimized distance between hydrophobic oCDs/AgNPs on the substrate surface played a crucial role in augmenting Raman signals. The concentration effects of hydrophobic oCDs/AgNPs within the hydrophobic SERS substrate led to the aggregation of target molecules in the hot spot region, further amplifying the SERS signal. This enhancement primarily stems from the generation of local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) on the rough surfaces of AgNPs, resulting in robust electromagnetic fields. He and coworkers provided a single molecule-level SERS platform via one-step synthesizing hydrophobic Ag/CDs on the gallium nitride (GaN) nanoflower [130]. The SERS sensor not only achieved single molecule detection with the enhancement factor of 2.483 × 109 but also provided long-term stability in the air for up to 8 months. The platform can achieve single-molecule-level detection of R6G molecules combined with statistical analysis, with LOD of 1 pM.

SPR sensors have advanced significantly in nanosensor technology and application over the last decade. SPR sensors are extremely useful in chemical and biological analyses because they can accurately observe and detect the targeted analytes and interactions over some time [160–162]. SPR sensors can detect molecular information in the electromagnetic region in a significant and sensitive manner [163]. Using Ag/CDs as an SPR sensor can be beneficial for various applications, including food quality analysis, medical diagnosis, and environmental monitoring [164,165]. The use of SPR as a sensor is more widely used by COL sensors where COL sensors usually use UV–Vis absorbance spectra to measure their detection capabilities. So, SPR is usually an integral part of COL sensors. Of all the literature reviewed, there is only one article reported the use of SPR itself. Masran and coworkers reported the application of Ag nanotriangular (NT)/CDs for the detection of creatinine based on a localized SPR biosensor [131]. Creatinine was detected through the H bonding between the functional group of the sensing material. In localized SPR, the AgNT/CDs exhibited the sensitivity of 4.07 nm (mg/dL)-1 respectively with LOD of 0.2 mg/dL.

Several works have been reported about Ag/CDs applications as dual-mode sensors towards chemical and biological samples including H2O2, urea, and lactate (FL/COL), uric acid (COL/SERS), Hg2+ (COL/EC), and tetracycline (FL/SPR). For instance, Elmasry and colleagues developed a dual-signal assay for urea detection in urine by synthesizing Ag/CDs [134]. The urease enzyme hydrolyzes urea to generate CO2 and NH3, leading to an increase in pH. This rise in pH activates the reduction affinity of tannic acid (TA), facilitating the in-situ generation of plasmonic AgNPs. The absorption spectra of AgNPs closely overlapped with the FL spectrum of CDs, allowing efficient quenching of the FL upon exposure to urea. The FL and COL dual signal was employed to quantify urea concentrations, with the LODs of 18 nM and 1.05 μM for FL and COL sensing, respectively. Figs. 13A & 13B UV–vis spectra and FL emission spectra of Ag/CDs after adding different urea concentrations. The color change from transparent to yellow was observed after increasing the urea concentration. As the pH increased due to NH3 release from urea decomposition, the absorption peak of AgNPs at 419 nm gradually increased, overlapping with the FL emission of N-CDs, resulting in a quenching effect based on the FRET mechanism. In addition, Walekar and colleagues used Ag/CDs as FL/COL sensors to detect H2O2 [87]. The FL intensity of Ag/CDs gradually increased in response to escalating H2O2 concentration (Fig. 13C). A strong linear correlation was observed within the H2O2 concentration range of 10 to 50 μM, yielding an LOD of 4.73 μM. A color change from yellow to colorless was observed with an increase in the concentration of H2O2 up to 50 μM (Fig. 13D). Regarding the sensing mechanism, the interaction of H2O2 with Ag/CDs induced the formation of Ag+ ions through the oxidation of Ag by H2O2 molecules. These Ag+ ions formed coordination bonds with the N atoms from CDs due to their high affinity, resulting in stable complexes within the aqueous solution.

Furthermore, Ag/CDs functioned as dual sensors, employing both COL and EC methods for detecting Hg2+ ions [118]. The composite displayed a noticeable shift from light yellow to colorless upon adding Hg2+ ions, with the absorbance gradually decreasing at 440 nm as concentrations varied from 10 to 50 μM. The naked eye discerned the color change, and the LOD was 0.5 μM. The colorimetric sensing mechanism involved enhancing the surface of AgNPs with CDs, containing diverse functional groups. Hg2+ ions attach to the composite surface via CDs, facilitating a redox reaction with AgNPs, resulting in the reduction of Hg2+ ions and oxidation of Ag0, leading to a colorless solution. Additionally, Ag/CDs served as an EC probe on a gold electrode (AuE) for Hg2+ ion detection. The modified AuE/Ag/CDs exhibited redox peaks in cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis within a potential range of −0.2 V to 0.5 V, unlike the bare electrode. When the electrode was solely modified with CDs, minimal peaks were observed in the CV, but the introduction of Hg2+ resulted in small redox peaks, indicating potential interaction between CDs and Hg2+ ions. The sensor's selectivity for Hg2+ ions was confirmed using CV, revealing a linear relationship between peak current and Hg2+ ion concentration, with an LOD of 0.004 nM within the 1–8 nM range. The synergistic interaction of CDs and AgNPs enabled EC detection of Hg2+ ions on an AuE modified with Ag/CDs, where the oxidation and reduction of Hg2+ ions correlated with redox peak appearance. CDs' surface functionalities engaged with Hg2+ ions through an ion exchange process, suggesting potential interactions between CDs and Hg2+ ions. The lower reduction potential of AgNPs compared to Hg2+ ions indicated a likely redox interaction between the two, with CDs facilitating this interaction.

Moreover, Ag/CDs were utilized as a detection tool for tetracycline (TC) in a study by Moaddab and Ghasemi [132]. Various concentrations of TC were individually introduced into solutions containing Ag/CDs. As the TC concentration increased, there was a gradual elevation in peak height, accompanied by a shift in the localized SPR (LSPR) peak location from 420 to 380 nm. A linear correlation between F/F0 and TC concentrations was established within the range of 0.025 to 3 ppm with LOD of 0.6 μg/L (1.2 nM). In the sensing mechanism, the proximity of AgNPs to TC led to an increase in FL intensity due to plasmon coupling between Ag and TC. AgNPs effectively improved the optical properties of TC through the metal-enhanced FL phenomenon. The closeness of Ag to TC increased their excitability and emission, resulting in an enhanced QY and photostability at an optimal distance ranging from 5 to 90 nm. When Ag and TC were at the right distance, energy transfer occurred between them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of Ag/CDs-based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Ag/CDs have unique features that offer several advantages. Ag/CDs exhibit strong FL, emitting light when exposed to external energy, which proves beneficial in sensors. They have large surface-to-volume ratios, providing more active sites for sensing (bio)molecules, resulting in increased sensitivity to detect analytes at low levels [166,167]. The combination of CDs and AgNPs results in highly quick, sensitive, and selective sensors for various applications, including biosensors, environmental sensors, and chemical sensors [168]. Engineered to be biocompatible, Ag/CDs are suitable for use in biological systems, making them valuable in biological research and medical diagnostics to detect biomolecules [169]. Through surface functionalization and modification, Ag/CDs can selectively bind with specific target molecules, enhancing sensor accuracy by reducing interferences from other substances [166,170]. Additionally, CDs also serve as protective coatings, preventing NPs from clumping and oxidizing, and ensuring long-term sensor stability [171]. Their production is generally affordable and cost-effective compared to other sensor technologies, with scalable synthesis procedures for mass production [172]. Tailoring the synthesis of Ag/CDs involves adjusting parameters such as precursor materials, synthesis methods, and reaction conditions, allowing control over the size, shape, and composition. Additionally, Ag/CDs can be environmentally friendly through the use of green synthesis techniques, distinguishing them from sensor technologies that may involve hazardous ingredients or processes.

Ag/CDs-based sensors have many advantages, but it is important to know that they also have some disadvantages. AgNPs have the potential to be toxic, especially in high concentrations or when exposed to living organisms over extended periods. Improper disposal of Ag/CDs could lead to the release of AgNPs into the environment [173]. This may pose risks to aquatic ecosystems and other organisms. Proper surface functionalization is necessary, and complex sample matrices may interfere with the sensor's performance [174]. The synthesis of high-quality Ag/CDs can be complex and require specific expertise and equipment. Achieving precise control over the Ag/CDs' size, shape, and composition can be challenging. Ag/CDs may be prone to aggregation, which can affect their properties and performance. The production of Ag/CDs may involve using expensive precursors and specialized equipment, which could lead to higher production costs than other materials. Depending on the intended applications, Ag/CDs may need to meet specific regulatory requirements and standards. Ag/CDs may compete with other advanced materials with similar or superior properties. Researchers and industries must carefully evaluate whether Ag/CDs offer the best solution for a given application. However, continuing studies attempt to address these problems and boost the functionality of Ag/CDs-based sensors. Fig. 14 depicts the summary of the advantages and disadvantages of Ag/CDs-based sensors.

CDs, known for their versatility, unique properties, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendly nature, find applications in diverse scientific and technological fields. The synthesis of CDs involves various methods, each offering distinct advantages, and precise adjustment of synthesis parameters is crucial for optimal results. CDs are promising candidates for developing nanocomposites, especially with AgNPs, leading to effective Ag/CDs nanocomposites. The importance of surface passivation, doping, and adjusting synthesis duration to enhance CDs' brightness and QY is emphasized. The synthesis of Ag/CDs is shifting towards environmentally sustainable approaches, utilizing CDs as green reducing, protecting, and capping agents. Various synthesis methods, characterization techniques, and comprehensive studies contribute to a deeper understanding of Ag/CDs' structure and properties, paving the way for continued advancements in advanced sensing applications. Ag/CDs have proven successful in chemical and biological sensing applications, serving as FL, EC, COL, SERS, and SPR sensors. Their adaptability is highlighted by their ability to detect analytes at low concentrations and participate in dual-mode sensing for diverse biomolecules and environmental pollutants. The advantageous features of strong FL, large surface-to-volume ratios, and biocompatibility make Ag/CDs valuable in biosensors and medical diagnostics. The sensing mechanisms employed by Ag/CDs for various analytes involve energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer, charge transfer phenomena, electrostatic interactions, and hydrogen bonding. These mechanisms contribute to discernible changes in the optical properties of Ag/CDs, showcasing their versatility and sensitivity in detecting a wide range of analytes for diverse applications.

Looking ahead, the future perspective for Ag/CDs in advanced sensing applications seems promising. The versatility, cost-effectiveness, and environmentally friendly nature of CDs set the stage for continued advancements. Synthesis methods for CDs and Ag/CDs are likely to undergo refinement, emphasizing brightness, QY, and reproducibility. The shift towards environmentally sustainable approaches aligns with the focus on green and scalable synthesis methods. Ag/CDs, with demonstrated success in various sensing modes and dual-mode applications, are poised to play a crucial role in detecting diverse analytes, offering adaptability and sensitivity. However, challenges like AgNPs toxicity, environmental concerns, and synthesis control difficulties need effective solutions for the continued success of Ag/CDs-based sensors. Future research may focus on comprehensive toxicity studies and advanced surface functionalization. Simplifying the synthesis process, optimizing parameters, and exploring innovative surface coating strategies are crucial for enhancing stability and reducing production costs. Continuous innovation and comparative studies will be critical for maintaining Ag/CDs' competitiveness. Staying informed about emerging materials and technologies will be key to identifying alternatives for advanced and sustainable sensing technologies. Overall, the future trajectory of CDs and Ag/CDs in sensing applications holds the promise of addressing current limitations and unlocking new possibilities.
